title_en: The Prophets from Idris to Ibrahim: An Era of Guidance
title_id: Para Nabi dari Idris hingga Ibrahim: Era Petunjuk
content_id:
Generasi-generasi antara Adam dan Ibrahim dibahas oleh Ibn Kathir melalui kisah-kisah beberapa nabi yang diutus kepada komunitas-komunitas tertentu, masing-masing membawa pesan esensial tauhid yang sama sementara menghadapi penyimpangan khusus kaum mereka. Idris (yang dalam tradisi Islam diidentifikasikan dengan Henokh) disebutkan secara singkat dalam Al-Quran sebagai seorang nabi yang diangkat ke kedudukan yang tinggi, dan Ibn Kathir menyajikan materi hadis dan tafsir tentang identitas dan eranya dengan kehati-hatian yang tepat mengenai keandalan riwayat-riwayat tambahan. Kisah Nuh menerima perlakuan paling luas di antara nabi-nabi pra-Ibrahim. Ibn Kathir mendokumentasikan misi kenabian Nuh selama sembilan ratus lima puluh tahun di antara kaumnya, penolakan keras kepala mereka, dan penghakiman ilahi yang akhirnya melalui banjir. Kisah Al-Quran tentang pembangunan bahtera, pengumpulan pasangan-pasangan binatang, dan banjir yang menenggelamkan seluruh bumi disajikan sebagai fakta sejarah yang didukung oleh berbagai ayat Al-Quran dan dikonfirmasi oleh riwayat-riwayat hadis yang menunjukkan bahwa banjir itu bersifat universal dalam cakupannya.

Komunitas yang selamat setelah banjir, keturunan dari putra-putra Nuh yang beriman, secara bertahap menyebar ke seluruh bumi dan membentuk peradaban manusia pasca-air bah. Ibn Kathir mencatat bahwa seluruh umat manusia setelah banjir adalah keturunan Nuh, menjadikannya dalam beberapa hal ayah kedua umat manusia, sebagaimana ditunjukkan oleh ayat Al-Quran bahwa keturunannyalah yang selamat. Generasi-generasi setelah Nuh akhirnya kembali ke syirik dan penyembahan berhala, dan Allah mengutus nabi-nabi selanjutnya sebagai respons. Hud diutus kepada kaum 'Ad, kaum Arab yang kuat di wilayah Yaman yang dikenal karena perawakan fisik mereka yang besar dan konstruksi yang mengesankan. Kesombongan dan penolakan mereka terhadap Hud menyebabkan kehancuran mereka oleh angin kencang yang berlangsung tujuh malam dan delapan hari, sebuah peristiwa yang direferensikan dalam berbagai surah Al-Quran. Salih diutus kepada kaum Tsamud, yang menuntut sebuah mukjizat dan diberi unta betina Allah sebagai tanda. Pembantaian yang disengaja terhadap unta itu dan penolakan selanjutnya terhadap Salih mendatangkan hukuman ilahi yang menghancurkan mereka sepenuhnya.

Nabi Luth, yang sezaman dengan Ibrahim, diutus kepada penduduk Sodom, yang telah terjerumus ke dalam dosa yang tidak pernah dilakukan oleh komunitas manapun sebelum mereka, khususnya praktik laki-laki mendekati laki-laki dengan hasrat seksual alih-alih perempuan. Al-Quran mencatat peringatan-peringatan Luth yang berkelanjutan, respons permusuhan kaum itu terhadapnya, dan kedatangan para utusan ilahi dalam wujud pemuda-pemuda tampan. Ketika Luth khawatir tentang para tamunya, para utusan itu mengungkapkan sifat asli mereka dan memberitahunya tentang hukuman ilahi yang akan datang. Luth dan keluarganya yang beriman diperintahkan untuk meninggalkan kota sebelum fajar, sementara istri Luth, yang bersimpati dengan para pelaku kesalahan, ditinggalkan. Kota-kota Sodom kemudian dibalikkan dan dihujani dengan batu-batu tanah liat yang dibakar. Ibn Kathir menerapkan metodologi standarnya di seluruh kisah-kisah ini, mengutip teks-teks Al-Quran sebagai otoritas utama, melengkapi dengan riwayat-riwayat hadis di mana tersedia, dan menandai Isra'iliyyat yang menambahkan detail-detail yang tidak ditemukan dalam sumber-sumber Islam primer.

Perlakuan Ibn Kathir terhadap era sejarah kenabian ini mencerminkan pemahamannya tentang rahmat ilahi dan keadilan ilahi sebagai atribut yang saling melengkapi daripada bertentangan. Masing-masing komunitas ini diberi waktu yang cukup, bukti yang cukup, dan seorang utusan kenabian yang jelas sebelum hukuman ilahi turun. Pola yang muncul, komunitas demi komunitas menolak bimbingan ilahi dan menanggung konsekuensinya, itu sendiri merupakan bentuk wahyu yang mendemonstrasikan konsistensi hukum ilahi lintas sejarah. Ibn Kathir mengambil pelajaran-pelajaran bagi pembacanya yang Muslim dari kisah-kisah ini, konsisten dengan niat Al-Quran dalam menyajikan narasi-narasi ini, menggunakannya untuk mendorong keteguhan dalam keimanan, memperingatkan terhadap kesombongan dan ketidaktaatan, dan menegaskan kemenangan akhir kebenaran ilahi atas penolakan manusia. Rantai kenabian dari Adam melalui Nuh, Hud, Salih, dan Luth mencapai pemenuhannya yang besar dalam figur Ibrahim, Kekasih Allah, yang menjadi subjek bagian berikutnya.
content_en:
The generations between Adam and Ibrahim are covered by Ibn Kathir through the accounts of several prophets sent to specific communities, each bearing the same essential message of tawhid while addressing the particular deviations of their people. Idris (identified in Islamic tradition with Enoch) is mentioned briefly in the Quran as a prophet raised to a high station, and Ibn Kathir presents the hadith and tafsir material on his identity and era with appropriate caution about the reliability of supplementary narrations. The account of Nuh (Noah) receives the most extensive treatment among the pre-Abrahamic prophets. Ibn Kathir documents Nuh's nine hundred and fifty years of prophetic mission among his people, their stubborn rejection, and the eventual divine judgment through the flood. The Quranic account of the construction of the Ark, the gathering of pairs of animals, and the flood that submerged the entire earth is presented as historical fact supported by multiple Quranic verses and confirmed by hadith narrations indicating that the flood was universal in extent.

The surviving community after the flood, descended from Nuh's believing sons, gradually spread across the earth and formed the post-diluvian human civilization. Ibn Kathir notes that all of humanity after the flood is descended from Nuh, making him in some respects a second father of the human race, as indicated by the Quranic verse that his progeny were the ones who survived. The generations after Nuh eventually fell back into shirk and idolatry, and Allah sent further prophets in response. Hud was sent to the people of 'Ad, a powerful Arab people in the region of Yemen known for their great physical stature and impressive construction. Their arrogance and rejection of Hud led to their destruction by a violent wind that lasted seven nights and eight days, an event referenced in multiple Quranic surahs. Salih was sent to the people of Thamud, who demanded a miracle and were given the she-camel of Allah as a sign. Their deliberate slaughter of the camel and subsequent rejection of Salih brought a divine punishment that destroyed them entirely.

The prophet Lut, contemporary of Ibrahim, was sent to the people of Sodom, who had fallen into a sin that no community before them had committed, specifically the practice of men approaching men with desire rather than women. The Quran records Lut's sustained warnings, the people's hostile response to him, and the arrival of the divine messengers in the form of young men of exceptional appearance. When Lut feared for his guests, the messengers revealed their true nature and informed him of the impending divine punishment. Lut and his believing family were commanded to leave the city before dawn, while Lut's wife, who had sympathized with the wrongdoers, was left behind. The cities of Sodom were then overturned and rained upon with stones of baked clay. Ibn Kathir applies his standard methodology throughout these accounts, citing the Quranic texts as primary authority, supplementing with hadith narrations where available, and flagging Isra'iliyyat that add details not found in the Islamic primary sources.

Ibn Kathir's treatment of this era of prophetic history reflects his understanding of divine mercy and divine justice as complementary rather than contradictory attributes. Each of these communities was given sufficient time, sufficient evidence, and a clear prophetic messenger before divine punishment fell. The pattern that emerges, community after community rejecting divine guidance and suffering the consequence, is itself a form of revelation demonstrating the consistency of divine law across history. Ibn Kathir draws lessons for his Muslim readers from these accounts, consistent with the Quranic intention in presenting these narratives, using them to encourage perseverance in faith, warn against arrogance and disobedience, and affirm the ultimate triumph of divine truth over human rejection. The prophetic chain from Adam through Nuh, Hud, Salih, and Lut reaches its great fulfillment in the figure of Ibrahim, the Friend of Allah, who forms the subject of the following section.
content_ar: The generations between آدم and إبراهيم ﷺ are covered by Ibn Kathir through the accounts of several الأنبياء sent to specific communities, each bearing the same الجوهري message of tawhid بينما addressing the particular deviations of their people. Idris (identified in Islamic التراث with Enoch) is mentioned briefly in the القرآن الكريم as a النبي raised to a high station, and Ibn Kathir presents the الحديث and التفسير material on his identity and era with appropriate caution about the reliability of supplementary narrations. The account of Nuh (نوح) receives the most extensive treatment among the pre-Abrahamic الأنبياء. Ibn Kathir documents Nuh's nine hundred and fifty years of prophetic mission among his people, their stubborn rejection, and the eventual divine judgment through the flood. The القرآني account of the construction of the Ark, the gathering of pairs of animals, and the flood that submerged the entire earth is presented as historical fact supported by multiple القرآني الآيات and confirmed by الحديث narrations indicating that the flood was universal in extent. The surviving الجماعة بعد the flood, descended from Nuh's believing sons, gradually spread across the earth and formed the post-diluvian human civilization. Ibn Kathir notes that all of humanity بعد the flood is descended from Nuh, making him in some respects a ثانياً الأب of the human race, as indicated by the القرآني الآية that his progeny were the ones who survived. The generations بعد Nuh eventually fell back into الشرك and idolatry, and الله sent further الأنبياء in response. Hud was sent to the people of 'Ad, a powerful Arab people in the region of Yemen known for their great physical stature and impressive construction. Their الغرور and rejection of Hud led to their destruction by a violent wind that lasted seven nights and eight days, an event referenced in multiple القرآني السور. Salih was sent to the people of Thamud, who demanded a miracle and were given the she-camel of الله as a sign. Their deliberate slaughter of the camel and subsequent rejection of Salih brought a divine العقاب that destroyed them entirely. النبي ﷺ Lut, contemporary of إبراهيم ﷺ, was sent to the people of Sodom, who had fallen into a الذنب that no الجماعة قبل them had committed, specifically the practice of men approaching men with desire rather than women. The القرآن الكريم records Lut's sustained warnings, the people's hostile response to him, and the arrival of the divine الرسل in the form of young men of exceptional appearance. عندما Lut feared for his guests, the الرسل revealed their true nature and informed him of the impending divine العقاب. Lut and his believing الأسرة were commanded to leave the city قبل dawn, بينما Lut's الزوجة, who had sympathized with the wrongdoers, was left behind.
